# UC-14.1.24 · Sensor Connectivity and Heartbeat Monitoring (Meraki MT)

## Description

Ensures all sensors maintain connectivity and operational status.

## Value

Ensures all sensors maintain connectivity and operational status.

## Implementation

Query sensor API for last report time. Alert on missing heartbeats.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Cisco Meraki Add-on for Splunk` (Splunkbase 5580).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=meraki:api`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Query sensor API for last report time. Alert on missing heartbeats.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=cisco_network sourcetype="meraki:api"
| stats latest(last_report) as last_checkin by sensor_id
| eval hours_since_checkin=round((now()-strptime(last_report, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"))/3600, 1)
| where hours_since_checkin > 2
```

Understanding this SPL

**Sensor Connectivity and Heartbeat Monitoring (Meraki MT)** — Ensures all sensors maintain connectivity and operational status.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=meraki:api`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Cisco Meraki Add-on for Splunk` (Splunkbase 5580). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: cisco_network; **sourcetype**: meraki:api. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=cisco_network, sourcetype="meraki:api". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by sensor_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **hours_since_checkin** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where hours_since_checkin > 2` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search in a known window. Spot-check a few events against the BMS console (for example Niagara, Tridium, or Honeywell) and confirm zones, values, and alarms line up. Verify field names and that the right roles can search the index.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Sensor status table; last heartbeat timeline; offline sensor list.

## SPL

```spl
index=cisco_network sourcetype="meraki:api"
| stats latest(last_report) as last_checkin by sensor_id
| eval hours_since_checkin=round((now()-strptime(last_report, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"))/3600, 1)
| where hours_since_checkin > 2
```

## Visualization

Sensor status table; last heartbeat timeline; offline sensor list.
